The ***Los Angeles Times*** is an American [daily newspaper](/wiki/Newspaper#Daily "Newspaper") that began publishing in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, in 1881.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Based in the [Greater Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles") city of [El Segundo](/wiki/El_Segundo,_California "El Segundo, California") since 2018,[[4]](#cite_note-4) it is the [sixth-largest newspaper in the U.S.](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_the_United_States "List of newspapers in the United States") and the largest in the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States") with a [print circulation](/wiki/Print_circulation "Print circulation") of 63,500.[[5]](#cite_note-5) As of 2022, it had 500,000 online subscribers, the fifth-largest among U.S. newspapers. Owned by [Patrick Soon-Shiong](/wiki/Patrick_Soon-Shiong "Patrick Soon-Shiong"), it is a subsidiary of the Los Angeles Times Media Group, and published by California Times, the paper has won over 40 [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prizes "Pulitzer Prizes") since its founding.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FrostWeingarten20172-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-caulfield1-9)

In the 19th century, the paper developed a reputation for [civic boosterism](/wiki/Civic_boosterism "Civic boosterism") and opposition to [labor unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"), the latter of which led to the [bombing of its headquarters](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_bombing "Los Angeles Times bombing") in 1910. The paper's profile grew substantially in the 1960s under publisher [Otis Chandler](/wiki/Otis_Chandler "Otis Chandler"), who adopted a more national focus. As with other regional newspapers in California[[10]](#cite_note-CaliforniaNewspapersFateSealed-10) and the United States, the paper's readership has declined since 2010. It has also been beset by a series of ownership changes, staff reductions, and other controversies.

In January 2018, the paper's staff voted to [unionize](/wiki/Unionize "Unionize") and finalized their first union contract on October 16, 2019.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The paper moved out of its [historic headquarters in downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Building "Los Angeles Times Building") to a facility in El Segundo, near [Los Angeles International Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport"), in July 2018. Since 2020, the newspaper's coverage has evolved away from national and international news and toward coverage of [California](/wiki/California "California") and especially [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") news.

In January 2024, the paper underwent its largest percentage reduction in headcount—a layoff exceeding 20 percent, including senior staff editorial positions—in an effort to stem the tide of financial losses and maintain enough cash to be viably operational through the end of the year in a struggle for survival and relevance as a regional newspaper of diminished status.[[12]](#cite_note-LATimesLayoffsSurvivalStruggle1-12)[[13]](#cite_note-LATimesLayoffsSurvivalStruggle2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-LATimesLayoffsSurvivalStruggle3-14) [Patrick Soon-Shiong](/wiki/Patrick_Soon-Shiong "Patrick Soon-Shiong"), who has owned the paper since 2018, announced in July 2025 that he would be taking the paper public within a year.[[15]](#cite_note-Regalado-15)

Since 2012, the LA Times has had a paywall and primarily generates revenue from monthly and annual subscriptions. The publication also generates significant revenue from display advertising, in addition to sponsored articles and press releases via Imperium Comms.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of *Los Angeles Times* publishers](/wiki/List_of_Los_Angeles_Times_publishers "List of Los Angeles Times publishers")

### Otis era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Otis era")]

Further information: [Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)](/wiki/Harrison_Gray_Otis_(publisher) "Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Photo-los-angeles-times-building-post-bombing.jpg/250px-Photo-los-angeles-times-building-post-bombing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photo-los-angeles-times-building-post-bombing.jpg)

Rubble of the [*Los Angeles Times* building](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_building "Los Angeles Times building") following the [1910 bombing](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_bombing "Los Angeles Times bombing")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Chandler_and_Otis_001.jpg/250px-Chandler_and_Otis_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chandler_and_Otis_001.jpg)

[Harry Chandler](/wiki/Harry_Chandler "Harry Chandler") and [Harrison Gray Otis](/wiki/Harrison_Gray_Otis_(publisher) "Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)") in August 1917

The *Times* was first published on December 4, 1881, as the *Los Angeles Daily Times*, under the direction of [Nathan Cole Jr.](/wiki/Nathan_Cole_Jr. "Nathan Cole Jr.") and [Thomas Gardiner](/wiki/Thomas_Gardiner_(publisher) "Thomas Gardiner (publisher)").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) It was first printed at the *Mirror* printing plant, which was then owned by [Jesse Yarnell](/wiki/Jesse_Yarnell "Jesse Yarnell") and [T. J. Caystile](/wiki/T._J._Caystile "T. J. Caystile"). Unable to pay the printing bill, Cole and Gardiner turned the paper over to the Mirror Company. In the meantime, [S. J. Mathes](/wiki/S._J._Mathes "S. J. Mathes") had joined the firm, and it was at his insistence that the *Times* continued publication. In July 1882, [Harrison Gray Otis](/wiki/Harrison_Gray_Otis_(publisher) "Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)") moved from [Santa Barbara, California](/wiki/Santa_Barbara,_California "Santa Barbara, California") to become the paper's editor.[[20]](#cite_note-LAT1923-20) At the same time he also purchased a 1/4 stake in the paper for $6,000 mostly secured on a bank loan.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Historian [Kevin Starr](/wiki/Kevin_Starr "Kevin Starr") wrote that Otis was a businessman "capable of manipulating the entire apparatus of politics and [public opinion](/wiki/Public_opinion "Public opinion") for his own enrichment".[[22]](#cite_note-22) Otis's editorial policy was based on [civic boosterism](/wiki/Boosterism "Boosterism"), extolling the virtues of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and promoting its growth. Toward those ends, the paper supported efforts to expand the city's water supply by [acquiring the rights to the water supply of the distant Owens Valley](/wiki/California_Water_Wars "California Water Wars").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The efforts of the *Times* to fight [local unions](/wiki/Organized_labor "Organized labor") led to the [bombing of its headquarters](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_bombing "Los Angeles Times bombing") on October 1, 1910, killing 21 people. Two of the union leaders, [James and Joseph McNamara](/wiki/McNamara_brothers "McNamara brothers"), were charged. The [American Federation of Labor](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Labor "American Federation of Labor") hired noted [trial attorney](/wiki/Trial_attorney "Trial attorney") [Clarence Darrow](/wiki/Clarence_Darrow "Clarence Darrow") to represent the brothers, who eventually pleaded guilty.

Otis fastened a bronze eagle on top of a high [frieze](/wiki/Frieze "Frieze") of the new *Times* headquarters building designed by [Gordon Kaufmann](/wiki/Gordon_Kaufmann "Gordon Kaufmann"), proclaiming anew the credo written by his wife, Eliza: "Stand Fast, Stand Firm, Stand Sure, Stand True".[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-answers.com-25)

### Chandler era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chandler era")]

Further information: [Harry Chandler](/wiki/Harry_Chandler "Harry Chandler"), [Norman Chandler](/wiki/Norman_Chandler "Norman Chandler"), and [Otis Chandler](/wiki/Otis_Chandler "Otis Chandler")

After Otis's death in 1917, his son-in-law and the paper's business manager, Harry Chandler, took control as publisher of the *Times*. Chandler was succeeded in 1944 by his son, Norman Chandler, who ran the paper during the rapid growth in Los Angeles following the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Norman's wife, [Dorothy Buffum Chandler](/wiki/Dorothy_Buffum_Chandler "Dorothy Buffum Chandler"), became active in civic affairs and led the effort to build the [Los Angeles Music Center](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Music_Center "Los Angeles Music Center"), whose main concert hall was named the [Dorothy Chandler Pavilion](/wiki/Dorothy_Chandler_Pavilion "Dorothy Chandler Pavilion") in her honor. Family members are buried at the [Hollywood Forever Cemetery](/wiki/Hollywood_Forever_Cemetery "Hollywood Forever Cemetery") near [Paramount Studios](/wiki/Paramount_Studios "Paramount Studios"). The site also includes a memorial to the Times Building bombing victims.

In 1935, the newspaper moved to a new, landmark Art Deco building, the [Los Angeles Times Building](/wiki/Times_Mirror_Square "Times Mirror Square"), to which the newspaper would add other facilities until taking up the entire city block between Spring, Broadway, First and Second streets, which came to be known as [Times Mirror Square](/wiki/Times_Mirror_Square "Times Mirror Square") and would house the paper until 2018. Harry Chandler, then the president and general manager of [Times-Mirror Co.](/wiki/Times_Mirror_Co. "Times Mirror Co."), declared the Los Angeles Times Building a "monument to the progress of our city and Southern California".[[26]](#cite_note-dimassa-26)

The fourth generation of family publishers, Otis Chandler, held that position from 1960 till 1980. Otis Chandler sought legitimacy and recognition for his family's paper, often forgotten in the power centers of the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") due to its geographic and cultural distance. He sought to remake the paper in the model of the nation's most respected newspapers, such as *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* and *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*. Believing that the newsroom was "the heartbeat of the business",[[27]](#cite_note-mcdougal-27) Otis Chandler increased the size and pay of the reporting staff and expanded its national and international reporting. In 1962, the paper joined with *The Washington Post* to form the [Los Angeles Times–Washington Post News Service](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times%E2%80%93Washington_Post_News_Service "Los Angeles Times–Washington Post News Service") to syndicate articles from both papers for other news organizations. He also toned down the unyielding [conservatism](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") that had characterized the paper over the years, adopting a much more centrist editorial stance.

During the 1960s, the paper won four [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), more than its previous nine decades combined.

In 2013, *Times* reporter Michael Hiltzik wrote that:

> The first generations bought or founded their local paper for profits and also social and political influence (which often brought more profits). Their children enjoyed both profits and influence, but as the families grew larger, the later generations found that only one or two branches got the power, and everyone else got a share of the money. Eventually the coupon-clipping branches realized that they could make more money investing in something other than newspapers. Under their pressure the companies went public, or split apart, or disappeared. That's the pattern followed over more than a century by the *Los Angeles Times* under the Chandler family.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The paper's early history and subsequent transformation were chronicled in an unauthorized history, *Thinking Big* (1977, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-399-11766-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-399-11766-0 "Special:BookSources/0-399-11766-0")), and were one of four organizations profiled by [David Halberstam](/wiki/David_Halberstam "David Halberstam") in *[The Powers That Be](/wiki/The_Powers_That_Be_(book) "The Powers That Be (book)")* (1979, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-394-50381-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-394-50381-3 "Special:BookSources/0-394-50381-3"); 2000 reprint [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-252-06941-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-252-06941-2 "Special:BookSources/0-252-06941-2")). Between the 1960s and the mid-2000s it was also the whole or partial subject of nearly thirty dissertations in communications and social science.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/1984-Newspaper-Vending-Machine.jpg/250px-1984-Newspaper-Vending-Machine.jpg)](/wiki/File:1984-Newspaper-Vending-Machine.jpg)

A *Times* [newspaper vending machine](/wiki/Newspaper_vending_machine "Newspaper vending machine") featuring news of the [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Los_Angeles_Times_building_El_Segundo_California_2023-06.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Times_building_El_Segundo_California_2023-06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Times_building_El_Segundo_California_2023-06.jpg)

The newspaper's current headquarters in [El Segundo, California](/wiki/El_Segundo,_California "El Segundo, California")

The *Los Angeles Times* was beset in the first decade of the 21st century by changes in ownership, a [bankruptcy](/wiki/Bankruptcy "Bankruptcy"), a rapid succession of editors, reductions in staff, decreases in paid circulation, the need to increase its Web presence, and a series of controversies.[[30]](#cite_note-wsj.com-30) In January 2024, the newsroom announced a roughly 20 percent reduction in staff, due to anemic subscription growth and other financial struggles.[[30]](#cite_note-wsj.com-30)

The newspaper moved to a new headquarters building in [El Segundo](/wiki/El_Segundo,_California "El Segundo, California"), near [Los Angeles International Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport"), in July 2018.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

#### Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ownership")]

In 2000, [Times Mirror Company](/wiki/Times_Mirror_Company "Times Mirror Company"), publisher of the *Los Angeles Times*, was purchased by the [Tribune Company](/wiki/Tribune_Company "Tribune Company") of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, placing the paper in co-ownership with the then WB-affiliated (now [CW](/wiki/CW_Television_Network "CW Television Network")-affiliated) [KTLA](/wiki/KTLA "KTLA"), which Tribune acquired in 1985.[[33]](#cite_note-Tribune-33)

On April 2, 2007, the Tribune Company announced its acceptance of real estate entrepreneur [Sam Zell](/wiki/Sam_Zell "Sam Zell")'s offer to buy the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")*, the *Los Angeles Times*, and all other company assets. Zell announced that he would sell the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") baseball club. He put up for sale the company's 25 percent interest in [Comcast SportsNet](/wiki/Comcast_SportsNet "Comcast SportsNet") Chicago. Until shareholder approval was received, Los Angeles billionaires [Ron Burkle](/wiki/Ron_Burkle "Ron Burkle") and [Eli Broad](/wiki/Eli_Broad "Eli Broad") had the right to submit a higher bid, in which case Zell would have received a $25 million buyout fee.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In December 2008, the Tribune Company filed for [bankruptcy protection](/wiki/Bankruptcy_protection "Bankruptcy protection"). The bankruptcy was a result of declining [advertising revenue](/wiki/Advertising_revenue "Advertising revenue") and a debt load of $12.9 billion, much of it incurred when the paper was taken private by Zell.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

On February 7, 2018, [Tribune Publishing](/wiki/Tribune_Publishing "Tribune Publishing"), formerly Tronc Inc., agreed to sell the *Los Angeles Times* and its two other [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") newspapers, *[The San Diego Union-Tribune](/wiki/The_San_Diego_Union-Tribune "The San Diego Union-Tribune")* and *[Hoy](/wiki/Hoy_(American_newspaper) "Hoy (American newspaper)")*, to billionaire biotech investor [Patrick Soon-Shiong](/wiki/Patrick_Soon-Shiong "Patrick Soon-Shiong").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The sale to Soon-Shiong through his Nant Capital investment fund, for $500 million plus the assumption of $90 million in pension liabilities,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-LAT_move-39) closed on June 16, 2018.[[40]](#cite_note-:0-40)

On July 21, 2025, Soon-Shiong announced while giving an interview on [The Daily Show](/wiki/The_Daily_Show "The Daily Show") that he would be taking the paper public within a year.[[15]](#cite_note-Regalado-15)

#### Editorial changes and staff reductions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Editorial changes and staff reductions")]

In 2000, [John Carroll](/wiki/John_Carroll_(journalist) "John Carroll (journalist)"), former editor of the *[Baltimore Sun](/wiki/Baltimore_Sun "Baltimore Sun")*, was brought in to restore the luster of the newspaper.[[41]](#cite_note-41) During his reign at the *Times*, he eliminated more than 200 jobs, but despite an operating profit margin of 20 percent, the Tribune executives were unsatisfied with returns, and by 2005 Carroll had left the newspaper. His successor, [Dean Baquet](/wiki/Dean_Baquet "Dean Baquet"), refused to impose the additional cutbacks mandated by the Tribune Company.

Baquet was the first African-American to hold this type of editorial position at a top-tier daily. During Baquet and Carroll's time at the paper, it won 13 [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), more than any other paper except *The New York Times*.[[42]](#cite_note-42) However, Baquet was removed from the editorship for not meeting the demands of the Tribune Group—as was publisher Jeffrey Johnson—and was replaced by James O'Shea of the *Chicago Tribune*. O'Shea himself left in January 2008 after a budget dispute with publisher [David Hiller](/wiki/David_Hiller "David Hiller").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The paper reported on July 3, 2008, that it planned to cut 250 jobs by [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day") and reduce the number of published pages by 15 percent.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) That included about 17 percent of the news staff, as part of the newly private media company's mandate to reduce costs.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Hiller himself resigned on July 14.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In January 2009, the *Times* eliminated the separate California/Metro section, folding it into the front section of the newspaper, and also announced seventy job cuts in news and editorial or a 10 percent cut in payroll.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In September 2015, [Austin Beutner](/wiki/Austin_Beutner "Austin Beutner"), the publisher and chief executive, was replaced by [Timothy E. Ryan](/wiki/Timothy_Ryan_(newspaper_publisher) "Timothy Ryan (newspaper publisher)").[[50]](#cite_note-NYT9915-50) On October 5, 2015, the [Poynter Institute](/wiki/Poynter_Institute "Poynter Institute") reported that "'At least 50' editorial positions will be culled from the *Los Angeles Times*" through a buyout.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Nancy Cleeland,[[52]](#cite_note-buyout-52) who took O'Shea's buyout offer, did so because of "frustration with the paper's coverage of working people and organized labor"[[53]](#cite_note-huffington-53) (the beat that earned her Pulitzer).[[52]](#cite_note-buyout-52) She speculated that the paper's revenue shortfall could be reversed by expanding coverage of [economic justice](/wiki/Economic_justice "Economic justice") topics, which she believed were increasingly relevant to Southern California; she cited the paper's attempted hiring of a "celebrity justice reporter" as an example of the wrong approach.[[53]](#cite_note-huffington-53)

On August 21, 2017, [Ross Levinsohn](/wiki/Ross_Levinsohn "Ross Levinsohn"), then aged 54, was named publisher and CEO, replacing [Davan Maharaj](/wiki/Davan_Maharaj "Davan Maharaj"), who had been both publisher and editor.[[54]](#cite_note-54) On June 16, 2018, the same day the sale to Patrick Soon-Shiong closed, [Norman Pearlstine](/wiki/Norman_Pearlstine "Norman Pearlstine") was named executive editor.[[40]](#cite_note-:0-40)

On May 3, 2021, the newspaper announced that it had selected [Kevin Merida](/wiki/Kevin_Merida "Kevin Merida") to be the new executive editor. Merida was then a senior vice president at [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and headed *[The Undefeated](/wiki/The_Undefeated_(website) "The Undefeated (website)")*, a site focused on sports, race, and culture; he had previously been the first Black managing editor at *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The *Los Angeles Times* Olympic Boulevard printing press was not purchased by Soon-Shiong and was kept by Tribune; in 2016 it was sold to developers who planned to build sound stages on the site.[[56]](#cite_note-56) It had opened in 1990 and could print 70,000 96-page newspapers an hour.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-last-58) The last issue of the *Times* printed at Olympic Boulevard was the March 11, 2024, edition.[[58]](#cite_note-last-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) Printing moved to [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_California "Riverside, California"), at the [Southern California News Group](/wiki/Southern_California_News_Group "Southern California News Group")'s *[Press-Enterprise](/wiki/The_Press-Enterprise "The Press-Enterprise")* printer, which also prints Southern California editions of the *New York Times* and *Wall Street Journal*.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In preparation for the printing plant closure and with a refocusing of sports coverage for editorial reasons, daily game coverage and box scores were eliminated on July 9, 2023. The sports section now features less time-sensitive articles, billed as similar to a magazine.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The change caused some consternation in the [Los Angeles Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Los_Angeles "History of the Jews in Los Angeles"), for many of whom reading box scores was a morning [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat") ritual.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

On January 23, 2024, the newspaper announced a layoff that would affect at least 115 employees.[[63]](#cite_note-63) It named Terry Tang its next executive editor on April 8, 2024.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

#### Circulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Circulation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/AbandonedLosAngelesTimesVendingMachine2011.jpg/250px-AbandonedLosAngelesTimesVendingMachine2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:AbandonedLosAngelesTimesVendingMachine2011.jpg)

An abandoned *Los Angeles Times* vending machine in [Covina, California](/wiki/Covina,_California "Covina, California"), in 2011

The *Times* has suffered continued decline in distribution. Reasons offered for the circulation drop included a price increase[[65]](#cite_note-65) and a rise in the proportion of readers preferring to read the online version instead of the print version.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Editor Jim O'Shea, in an internal memo announcing a May 2007, mostly voluntary, [reduction in force](/wiki/Reduction_in_force "Reduction in force"), characterized the decrease in circulation as an "industry-wide problem" which the paper had to counter by "growing rapidly on-line", "break[ing] news on the Web and explain[ing] and analyz[ing] it in our newspaper."[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The *Times* closed its [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley") printing plant in early 2006, leaving press operations to the Olympic plant and to [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California"). Also that year the paper announced its circulation had fallen to 851,532, down 5.4 percent from 2005. The *Times*'s loss of circulation was the largest of the top ten newspapers in the U.S.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Some observers believed that the drop was due to the retirement of circulation director Bert Tiffany. Others thought the decline was a side effect of a succession of short-lived editors who were appointed by publisher Mark Willes after publisher [Otis Chandler](/wiki/Otis_Chandler "Otis Chandler") relinquished day-to-day control in 1995.[[27]](#cite_note-mcdougal-27) Willes, the former president of [General Mills](/wiki/General_Mills "General Mills"), was criticized for his lack of understanding of the newspaper business, and was derisively referred to by reporters and editors as *The Cereal Killer*.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Subsequently, the Orange County plant closed in 2010.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The *Times*'s reported daily circulation in October 2010 was 600,449,[[71]](#cite_note-71) down from a peak of 1,225,189 daily and 1,514,096 Sunday in April 1990.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) By 2024, print circulation was 79,000.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

#### Internet presence and free weeklies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Internet presence and free weeklies")]

In December 2006, a team of *Times* reporters delivered management with a critique of the paper's online news efforts known as the Spring Street Project.[[75]](#cite_note-Spring_Street_Transcript-75) The report, which condemned the *Times* as a "web-stupid" organization,[[75]](#cite_note-Spring_Street_Transcript-75) was followed by a shakeup in management of the paper's website,[[76]](#cite_note-Spring_Street_Hiller_Memo-76) and a rebuke of print staffers who were described as treating "change as a threat."[[77]](#cite_note-Spring_Street_O'Shea_Speech-77)

On July 10, 2007, the *Times* launched a local [Metromix](/wiki/Metromix "Metromix") site targeting live entertainment for young adults.[[78]](#cite_note-78) A free weekly [tabloid](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") print edition of Metromix Los Angeles followed in February 2008; the publication was the newspaper's first stand-alone print weekly.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In 2009, the *Times* shut down Metromix and replaced it with *Brand X*, a [blog site](/wiki/Blog_site "Blog site") and free weekly tabloid targeting young, [social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") readers.[[80]](#cite_note-80) *Brand X* launched in March 2009; the *Brand X* tabloid ceased publication in June 2011 and the website was shut down the following month.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

In May 2018, the *Times* blocked access to its online edition from most of Europe because of the European Union's [General Data Protection Regulation](/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation "General Data Protection Regulation").[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Gaza war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gaza war")]

See also: [Media coverage of the Gaza war](/wiki/Media_coverage_of_the_Gaza_war "Media coverage of the Gaza war")

According to an early 2024 analysis by *[The Intercept](/wiki/The_Intercept "The Intercept"),* the *Los Angeles Times,* along with other U.S.-based newspapers, exhibited a consistent bias against [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") in their coverage of the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"). The study, which examined over 1,000 headlines from multiple U.S. newspapers during the first six weeks of the war, was obtained by "searching for all articles that contained relevant words (such as “Palestinian,” “Gaza,” “Israeli,” etc.) on three news websites." The analysis claims that the outlets disproportionately emphasized Israeli deaths, used emotive language to describe Israeli casualties but not Palestinian ones, and focused more on [antisemitism in the U.S.](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_United_States "Antisemitism in the United States") than on [anti-Muslim discrimination](/wiki/Islamophobia_in_the_United_States "Islamophobia in the United States"). Additionally, despite the high Palestinian death toll, their suffering was underreported compared to coverage of similar events that took place during the [war in Ukraine](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"). The [bias in media](/wiki/Media_bias_in_the_United_States "Media bias in the United States") representation influences public perception and U.S. political support for [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), even as younger audiences increasingly turn to social media for alternative narratives.[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*][[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Other incidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other incidents")]

Further information: [Andrés Martinez (editor) § .22Grazergate.22 Controversy](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Martinez_(editor)#.22Grazergate.22_Controversy "Andrés Martinez (editor)"), and [News media endorsements in the 2024 United States presidential election § Suppression of Harris endorsements](/wiki/News_media_endorsements_in_the_2024_United_States_presidential_election#Suppression_of_Harris_endorsements "News media endorsements in the 2024 United States presidential election")

In 1999, it was revealed that a revenue-sharing arrangement was in place between the *Times* and [Staples Center](/wiki/Staples_Center "Staples Center") in the preparation of a 168-page magazine about the opening of the sports arena. The magazine's editors and writers were not informed of the agreement, which breached the [Chinese wall](/wiki/Chinese_wall "Chinese wall") that traditionally has separated advertising from journalistic functions at American newspapers. Publisher Mark Willes also had not prevented advertisers from pressuring reporters in other sections of the newspaper to write stories favorable to their point of view.[[87]](#cite_note-87)
[Michael Kinsley](/wiki/Michael_Kinsley "Michael Kinsley") was hired as the Opinion and Editorial ([op-ed](/wiki/Op-ed "Op-ed")) Editor in April 2004 to help improve the quality of the opinion pieces. His role was controversial, for he forced writers to take a more decisive stance on issues. In 2005, he created a [Wikitorial](/wiki/Wikitorial "Wikitorial"), the first [Wiki](/wiki/Wiki "Wiki") by a major news organization. Although it failed, readers could combine forces to produce their own editorial pieces. It was shut down after being besieged with inappropriate material. He resigned later that year.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

In 2003, the *Times* drew fire for a last-minute story before the [California recall election](/wiki/California_recall_election,_2003 "California recall election, 2003") alleging that [gubernatorial](/wiki/Gubernatorial "Gubernatorial") candidate [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") groped scores of women during his movie career. Columnist [Jill Stewart](/wiki/Jill_Stewart "Jill Stewart") wrote on the *American Reporter* website that the *Times* did not do a story on allegations that former Governor [Gray Davis](/wiki/Gray_Davis "Gray Davis") had verbally and physically abused women in his office, and that the Schwarzenegger story relied on a number of anonymous sources. Further, she said, four of the six alleged victims were not named. She also said that in the case of the Davis allegations, the *Times* decided against printing the Davis story because of its reliance on anonymous sources.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) The [American Society of Newspaper Editors](/wiki/American_Society_of_Newspaper_Editors "American Society of Newspaper Editors") said that the *Times* lost more than 10,000 subscribers because of the negative publicity surrounding the Schwarzenegger article.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

On November 12, 2005, new op-ed editor [Andrés Martinez](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Martinez_(editor) "Andrés Martinez (editor)") announced the dismissal of liberal op-ed columnist [Robert Scheer](/wiki/Robert_Scheer "Robert Scheer") and conservative editorial cartoonist [Michael Ramirez](/wiki/Michael_Ramirez "Michael Ramirez").[[92]](#cite_note-92) The *Times* also came under controversy for its decision to drop the weekday edition of the *[Garfield](/wiki/Garfield "Garfield")* comic strip in 2005, in favor of a hipper comic strip *[Brevity](/wiki/Brevity_(comic_strip) "Brevity (comic strip)")*, while retaining it in the Sunday edition. *Garfield* was dropped altogether shortly thereafter.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Following the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")'s defeat in the [2006 mid-term elections](/wiki/United_States_elections,_2006 "United States elections, 2006"), an Opinion piece by [Joshua Muravchik](/wiki/Joshua_Muravchik "Joshua Muravchik"), a leading [neoconservative](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism") and a resident scholar at the conservative [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute"), published on November 19, 2006, was titled 'Bomb Iran'. The article shocked some readers, with its hawkish comments in support of more unilateral action by the United States, this time against Iran.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

On March 22, 2007, editorial page editor Andrés Martinez resigned following an alleged scandal centering on his girlfriend's professional relationship with a Hollywood producer who had been asked to guest-edit a section in the newspaper.[[95]](#cite_note-95) In an open letter written upon leaving the paper, Martinez criticized the publication for allowing the Chinese wall between the news and editorial departments to be weakened, accusing news staffers of lobbying the opinion desk.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In November 2017, [Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)") blacklisted the *Times* from attending press screenings of its films, in retaliation for September 2017 reportage by the paper on [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney")'s political influence in the Anaheim area. The company considered the coverage to be "biased and inaccurate". As a sign of condemnation and solidarity, a number of major publications and writers, including *The New York Times*, *[Boston Globe](/wiki/Boston_Globe "Boston Globe")* critic [Ty Burr](/wiki/Ty_Burr "Ty Burr"), *[Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post")* blogger Alyssa Rosenberg, and the websites *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* and *[Flavorwire](/wiki/Flavorwire "Flavorwire")*, announced that they would boycott press screenings of future Disney films. The [National Society of Film Critics](/wiki/National_Society_of_Film_Critics "National Society of Film Critics"), [Los Angeles Film Critics Association](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Film_Critics_Association "Los Angeles Film Critics Association"), [New York Film Critics Circle](/wiki/New_York_Film_Critics_Circle "New York Film Critics Circle"), and [Boston Society of Film Critics](/wiki/Boston_Society_of_Film_Critics "Boston Society of Film Critics") jointly announced that Disney's films would be ineligible for their respective year-end awards unless the decision was reversed, condemning the decision as being "antithetical to the principles of a free press and [setting] a dangerous precedent in a time of already heightened hostility towards journalists". On November 7, 2017, Disney reversed its decision, stating that the company "had productive discussions with the newly installed leadership at the *Los Angeles Times* regarding our specific concerns".[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In October 2024, Soon-Shiong, the owner of the *Times*, told executive editor Terry Tang that the newspaper must not endorse a candidate in the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), but should instead print "a factual analysis of all the POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE policies by EACH candidate during their tenures at the White House, and how these policies affected the nation". The *Times* editorial board, which had been preparing to endorse [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") presidential candidate, rejected this alternative to endorsement, and after [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), the Republican candidate, alluded to the newspaper not having endorsed Harris, Mariel Garza, the editor of the opinion section, resigned in protest, as did two other members of the editorial board, Robert Greene and Karin Klein.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) Two hundred *Times* staff signed a letter condemning the way in which the non-endorsement was handled, and thousands of subscribers cancelled their subscriptions.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Soon-Shiong had previously blocked an endorsement by the editorial board in 2020, when he overruled their decision to endorse [Elizabeth Warren](/wiki/Elizabeth_Warren "Elizabeth Warren") in the [2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2020_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries").[[104]](#cite_note-104)

Following the election, Soon-Shiong stated that he plans to add an AI-powered "bias meter" to all of the paper's articles allowing readers to access "both sides" of stories.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Amidst Soon-Shiong's public display of support for [Robert F. Kennedy Jr.](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Jr. "Robert F. Kennedy Jr."), Trump's pick for leader of the [US Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/US_Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "US Department of Health and Human Services"), opinion columnist Eric Reinhart alleged the paper cut a critical piece he wrote about Kennedy.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

## Pulitzer Prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pulitzer Prizes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Tragedy_by_the_Sea.jpg/250px-Tragedy_by_the_Sea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tragedy_by_the_Sea.jpg)

*[Tragedy by the Sea](/wiki/Tragedy_by_the_Sea "Tragedy by the Sea")*, an April 1954 photo taken by *Los Angeles Times* photographer [John L. Gaunt](/wiki/John_L._Gaunt "John L. Gaunt") of a young couple standing together beside the Pacific Ocean in [Hermosa Beach, California](/wiki/Hermosa_Beach,_California "Hermosa Beach, California"). A few minutes before the image was taken, the couple's 19-month-old son Michael disappeared. The photo won the 1955 [Pulitzer Prize for Photography](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Photography "Pulitzer Prize for Photography").

As of 2024, the *Times* has won 41 [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), including four in editorial cartooning, and one each in spot news reporting for the 1965 [Watts Riots](/wiki/Watts_Riots "Watts Riots") and the [1992 Los Angeles riots](/wiki/1992_Los_Angeles_riots "1992 Los Angeles riots").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

* The *Los Angeles Times* photographer [John L. Gaunt Jr.](/wiki/John_L._Gaunt_Jr. "John L. Gaunt Jr.")'s photo *[Tragedy by the Sea](/wiki/Tragedy_by_the_Sea "Tragedy by the Sea")* *(pictured)* won the 1955 [Pulitzer Prize for Photography](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Photography "Pulitzer Prize for Photography"). The image was published April 3, 1954.[[108]](#cite_note-Beach-108)
* The *Los Angeles Times* received the [1984](/wiki/1984_Pulitzer_Prize "1984 Pulitzer Prize") [Pulitzer Prize for Public Service](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Public_Service "Pulitzer Prize for Public Service") for the newspaper series "[Latinos](/wiki/Latinos_(newspaper_series) "Latinos (newspaper series)")".[[109]](#cite_note-109)
* *Times* sportswriter [Jim Murray](/wiki/Jim_Murray_(sportswriter) "Jim Murray (sportswriter)") won a Pulitzer in 1990.
* *Times* investigative reporters [Chuck Philips](/wiki/Chuck_Philips "Chuck Philips") and [Michael Hiltzik](/wiki/Michael_Hiltzik "Michael Hiltzik") won the Pulitzer in 1999[[110]](#cite_note-1999_Pulitzer_prize_winners_for_beat_reporting-110) for a year-long series that exposed corruption in the music business.[[111]](#cite_note-2_times_staffers_honored-111)
* *Times* journalist [David Willman](/wiki/David_Willman "David Willman") won the 2001 [Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Reporting](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Investigative_Reporting "Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Reporting"); the organization cited "his pioneering expose of seven unsafe prescription drugs that had been approved by the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration"), and an analysis of the policy reforms that had reduced the agency's effectiveness."[[112]](#cite_note-112) In [2004](/wiki/2004_Pulitzer_Prize "2004 Pulitzer Prize"), the paper won five prizes, which is the third-most by any paper in one year (behind *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* in [2002](/wiki/2002_Pulitzer_Prize "2002 Pulitzer Prize") (7) and *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* in [2008](/wiki/2008_Pulitzer_Prize "2008 Pulitzer Prize") (6)).
* *Times* reporters Bettina Boxall and Julie Cart won a Pulitzer Prize for Explanatory Reporting in 2009 "for their fresh and painstaking exploration into the cost and effectiveness of attempts to combat the growing menace of wildfires across the western United States."[[113]](#cite_note-113)
* In 2011, [Barbara Davidson](/wiki/Barbara_Davidson "Barbara Davidson") was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Feature Photography "for her intimate story of innocent victims trapped in the city's crossfire of deadly gang violence."[[114]](#cite_note-114)
* In 2016, the *Times* won the breaking news Pulitzer prize for its coverage of the [mass shooting in](/wiki/2015_San_Bernardino_attack "2015 San Bernardino attack") [San Bernardino](/wiki/San_Bernardino "San Bernardino"), California.[[115]](#cite_note-115)
* In 2019, three *Los Angeles Times* reporters, [Harriet Ryan](/wiki/Harriet_Ryan "Harriet Ryan"), Matt Hamilton and [Paul Pringle](/wiki/Paul_Pringle "Paul Pringle"), won a Pulitzer Prize for their investigation into a [gynecologist accused of abusing hundreds of students at the University of Southern California](/wiki/George_Tyndall "George Tyndall").[[116]](#cite_note-116)
* In 2020, [Christopher Knight](/wiki/Christopher_Knight_(art_critic) "Christopher Knight (art critic)") won the Pulitzer Prize for Criticism, "For work demonstrating extraordinary community service by a critic, applying his expertise and enterprise to critique a proposed overhaul of the L.A. County Museum of Art and its effect on the institution's mission."[[117]](#cite_note-117)

## Competition and rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Competition and rivalries")]

In the 19th century, the chief competition to the *Times* was the *[Los Angeles Examiner](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Herald_Examiner "Los Angeles Herald Examiner")* followed by the smaller *[Los Angeles Tribune](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Tribune_(1886%E2%80%931890) "Los Angeles Tribune (1886–1890)").* In December 1903, newspaper magnate [William Randolph Hearst](/wiki/William_Randolph_Hearst "William Randolph Hearst") began publishing the *Los Angeles Examiner* as a direct morning competitor to the *Times.*[[118]](#cite_note-118) In the 20th century, the *[Los Angeles Express](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Herald-Examiner "Los Angeles Herald-Examiner")*, [Manchester Boddy](/wiki/Manchester_Boddy "Manchester Boddy")'s [Los Angeles *Daily News*](/wiki/Illustrated_Daily_News "Illustrated Daily News"), a Democratic newspaper, were both afternoon competitors.[[119]](#cite_note-RedInk-119)

By the mid-1940s, the *Times* was the leading newspaper in terms of circulation in the [Greater Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles"). In 1948, it launched the *Los Angeles Mirror*, an afternoon tabloid, to compete with both the *Daily News* and the merged *Herald-Express*. In 1954, the *Mirror* absorbed the *Daily News*. The combined paper, the *Mirror-News*, ceased publication in 1962, when the Hearst afternoon *[Herald-Express](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Herald_Examiner "Los Angeles Herald Examiner")* and the morning *[Los Angeles Examiner](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Examiner "Los Angeles Examiner")* merged to become the *[Herald-Examiner](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Herald-Examiner "Los Angeles Herald-Examiner")*.[[120]](#cite_note-Pitt-120) The *Herald-Examiner* published its last number in 1989.

In 2014, the *Los Angeles Register*, published by Freedom Communications, then-parent company of the *[Orange County Register](/wiki/Orange_County_Register "Orange County Register")*, was launched as a daily newspaper to compete with the *Times*. By late September of that year, however, the *Los Angeles Register* closed.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

## Special editions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Special editions")]

### Midwinter and midsummer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Midwinter and midsummer")]

#### Midwinter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Midwinter")]

For 69 years, from 1885[[123]](#cite_note-123) until 1954, the *Times* issued on New Year's Day a special annual Midwinter Number or Midwinter Edition that extolled the virtues of Southern California. At first, it was called the "Trade Number", and in 1886 it featured a special press run of "extra scope and proportions"; that is, "a twenty-four-page paper, and we hope to make it the finest exponent of this [Southern California] country that ever existed."[[124]](#cite_note-124) Two years later, the edition had grown to "forty-eight handsome pages (9×15 inches), [which] stitched for convenience and better preservation", was "equivalent to a 150-page book."[[125]](#cite_note-125) The last use of the phrase *Trade Number* was in 1895, when the edition had grown to thirty-six pages split among three separate sections.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

The Midwinter Number drew acclamations from other newspapers, including this one from *[The Kansas City Star](/wiki/The_Kansas_City_Star "The Kansas City Star")* in 1923:

> It is made up of five magazines with a total of 240 pages – the maximum size possible under the postal regulations. It goes into every detail of information about Los Angeles and Southern California that the heart could desire. It is virtually a cyclopedia on the subject. It drips official statistics. In addition, it verifies the statistics with a profusion of illustration. . . . it is a remarkable combination of guidebook and travel magazine.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

In 1948, the Midwinter Edition, as it was then called, had grown to "7 big picture magazines in beautiful [rotogravure](/wiki/Rotogravure "Rotogravure") reproduction."[[128]](#cite_note-128) The last mention of the Midwinter Edition was in a *Times* advertisement on January 10, 1954.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

#### Midsummer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Midsummer")]

Between 1891 and 1895, the *Times* also issued a similar Midsummer Number, the first one featuring the theme, "The Land and Its Fruits".[[130]](#cite_note-130) Because of its issue date in September, the edition was in 1891 called the Midsummer Harvest Number.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Zoned editions and subsidiaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Zoned editions and subsidiaries")]

Main article: [*Los Angeles Times* suburban sections](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_suburban_sections "Los Angeles Times suburban sections")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Avalon_Wireless_front_page_-_25MAR1903.jpg/250px-Avalon_Wireless_front_page_-_25MAR1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avalon_Wireless_front_page_-_25MAR1903.jpg)

Front page of the March 25, 1903, debut issue of the short-lived *The Wireless*, published in [Avalon](/wiki/Avalon,_California "Avalon, California")[[132]](#cite_note-132)

In 1903, Pacific Wireless Telegraph Company established a radiotelegraph link between the California mainland and [Santa Catalina Island](/wiki/Santa_Catalina_Island,_California "Santa Catalina Island, California"). In the summer of that year, the *Times* made use of this link to establish a local daily paper, based in [Avalon](/wiki/Avalon,_California "Avalon, California"), *The Wireless*, which featured local news plus excerpts which had been transmitted via Morse code from the parent paper.[[133]](#cite_note-133) However, this effort apparently survived for only a little more than one year.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

In the 1990s, the *Times* published various editions catering to far-flung areas. Editions included those from the San Fernando Valley, [Ventura County](/wiki/Ventura_County,_California "Ventura County, California"), [Inland Empire](/wiki/Inland_Empire,_California "Inland Empire, California"), [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California"), [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County,_California "San Diego County, California") & a "National Edition" that was distributed to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"). Overall, there were 14 editions succeeded by *Our Times*, a group of community supplements included in editions of the regular *Los Angeles Metro* newspaper, with the *Our Times* editions ceasing publication in 2000.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

A subsidiary, Times Community Newspapers, publishes the *[Daily Pilot](/wiki/Daily_Pilot "Daily Pilot")* of [Newport Beach](/wiki/Newport_Beach,_California "Newport Beach, California") and [Costa Mesa](/wiki/Costa_Mesa,_California "Costa Mesa, California").[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) From 2011 to 2013, the *Times* had published the *Pasadena Sun*.[[138]](#cite_note-138) It also had published the *[Glendale News-Press](/wiki/Glendale_News-Press "Glendale News-Press")* and *[Burbank Leader](/wiki/Burbank_Leader "Burbank Leader")* from 1993 to 2020, and the *La Cañada Valley Sun* from 2005 to 2020.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

On April 30, 2020, Charlie Plowman, publisher of Outlook Newspapers, announced he would acquire the *Glendale News-Press*, *Burbank Leader* and *La Cañada Valley Sun* from Times Community Newspapers. Plowman acquired the *South Pasadena Review* and *San Marino Tribune* in late January 2020 from the Salter family, who owned and operated these two community weeklies.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Features")]

One of the *Times*' features was "Column One", a feature that appeared daily on the front page to the left-hand side. Established in September 1968, it was a place for the weird and the interesting; in the *How Far Can a Piano Fly?* (a compilation of Column One stories) introduction, [Patt Morrison](/wiki/Patt_Morrison "Patt Morrison") wrote that the column's purpose was to elicit a "Gee, that's interesting, I didn't know that" type of reaction.

The *Times* also embarked on a number of [investigative journalism](/wiki/Investigative_journalism "Investigative journalism") pieces. A series in December 2004 on the [King/Drew Medical Center](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Outpatient_Center "Martin Luther King Jr. Outpatient Center") in Los Angeles led to a Pulitzer Prize and a more thorough coverage of the hospital's troubled history. Lopez wrote a five-part series on the civic and humanitarian disgrace of Los Angeles' [Skid Row](/wiki/Skid_Row,_Los_Angeles "Skid Row, Los Angeles"), which became the focus of a 2009 motion picture, *[The Soloist](/wiki/The_Soloist "The Soloist").* The paper also won 75 awards at the 2020 [Society for News Design](/wiki/Society_for_News_Design "Society for News Design") (SND) awards for work completed in 2019.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

From 1967 to 1972, the *Times* produced a Sunday [supplement](/wiki/Supplement_(publishing) "Supplement (publishing)") called *[West](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Magazine#West_magazine "Los Angeles Times Magazine")* magazine. *West* was recognized for its art design, which was directed by Mike Salisbury (who later became art director of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine).[[142]](#cite_note-Heller-142) From 2000 to 2012, the *Times* published the *[Los Angeles Times Magazine](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Magazine "Los Angeles Times Magazine")*, which started as a weekly and then became a monthly supplement. The magazine focused on stories and photos of people, places, style, and other cultural affairs occurring in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and its surrounding cities and communities. In 2014, *[The California Sunday Magazine](/wiki/The_California_Sunday_Magazine "The California Sunday Magazine")* was included in the Sunday *L.A. Times* edition, but stopped publishing in 2020.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

In 2024, the *Times* published an "L.A. Influential" series, featuring the city's most prominent moguls, artists, community leaders, and others.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) The feature is arranged in six categories, based on industry and other details.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

### Festival of Books

Main article: [Los Angeles Times Festival of Books](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Festival_of_Books "Los Angeles Times Festival of Books")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Fest_of_Books_2009.jpg/250px-Fest_of_Books_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fest_of_Books_2009.jpg)

The [*Los Angeles Times* Festival of Books](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Festival_of_Books "Los Angeles Times Festival of Books") in 2009, held on the [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") campus

In 1996, the *Times* started the annual [*Los Angeles Times* Festival of Books](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Festival_of_Books "Los Angeles Times Festival of Books"), in association with the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"). It has panel discussions, exhibits, and stages during two days at the end of April each year.[[147]](#cite_note-147) In 2011, the Festival of Books was moved to the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California").[[148]](#cite_note-148)

### Book prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Book prizes")]

Main article: [Los Angeles Times Book Prize](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Book_Prize "Los Angeles Times Book Prize")

Since 1980, the *Times* has awarded annual book prizes. The categories are now biography, current interest, fiction, first fiction, history, mystery/thriller, poetry, science and technology, and young adult fiction. In addition, the [Robert Kirsch Award](/wiki/Robert_Kirsch_Award "Robert Kirsch Award") is presented annually to a living author with a substantial connection to the American West whose contribution to American letters deserves special recognition".[[149]](#cite_note-149)

Main article: [Los Angeles Times 500](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_500 "Los Angeles Times 500")

From 1974 to 1980, the *Times* sponsored the **Los Angeles Times 500**, a [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") [Winston Cup Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Cup_Series "NASCAR Cup Series") [stock car race](/wiki/Stock_car_racing "Stock car racing") that was held at [Ontario Motor Speedway](/wiki/Ontario_Motor_Speedway "Ontario Motor Speedway") in [Ontario, California](/wiki/Ontario,_California "Ontario, California"), the final event was held in 1980 before the track was demolished.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Los Angeles Times Grand Prix")]

Main article: [Los Angeles Times Grand Prix](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Grand_Prix "Los Angeles Times Grand Prix")

From 1957 to 1987, the *Times* sponsored the [Los Angeles Times Grand Prix](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times_Grand_Prix "Los Angeles Times Grand Prix") that was held at the [Riverside International Raceway](/wiki/Riverside_International_Raceway "Riverside International Raceway") in [Moreno Valley, California](/wiki/Moreno_Valley,_California "Moreno Valley, California").

## Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Other media")]

### Book publishing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Book publishing")]

The Times Mirror Corporation has also owned a number of book publishers over the years, including [New American Library](/wiki/New_American_Library "New American Library"), [C.V. Mosby Company](/wiki/Mosby_(imprint) "Mosby (imprint)"), [Harry N. Abrams](/wiki/Harry_N._Abrams "Harry N. Abrams"), [Matthew Bender](/wiki/Matthew_Bender "Matthew Bender"), and [Jeppesen](/wiki/Jeppesen "Jeppesen").[[150]](#cite_note-NAL-150)

In 1960, Times Mirror of Los Angeles bought the book publisher New American Library, known for publishing affordable paperback reprints of classics and other scholarly works.[[151]](#cite_note-151) The NAL continued to operate autonomously from New York and within the Mirror Company. In 1983, Odyssey Partners and Ira J. Hechler bought NAL from the Times Mirror Company for over $50 million.[[150]](#cite_note-NAL-150)

In 1967, Times Mirror acquired C.V. Mosby, a professional publisher and merged it over the years with several other professional publishers including Resource Application, Inc., Year Book Medical Publishers, Wolfe Publishing Ltd., PSG Publishing Company, B.C. Decker, Inc., among others. Eventually in 1998 Mosby was sold to Harcourt Brace & Company to form the Elsevier Health Sciences group.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

The Times-Mirror Company was a founding owner of television station [KTTV](/wiki/KTTV "KTTV") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), which opened in January 1949. It became that station's sole owner in 1951, after re-acquiring the minority shares it had sold to [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") in 1948. Times-Mirror also purchased a former motion picture studio, [Nassour Studios](/wiki/Nassour_Studios "Nassour Studios"), in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles,_California "Hollywood, Los Angeles, California") in 1950, which was then used to consolidate KTTV's operations. Later to be known as [Metromedia Square](/wiki/Metromedia_Square "Metromedia Square"), the studio was sold along with KTTV to [Metromedia](/wiki/Metromedia "Metromedia") in 1963.

After a seven-year hiatus from the medium, the firm reactivated **Times-Mirror Broadcasting Company** with its 1970 purchase of the *[Dallas Times Herald](/wiki/Dallas_Times_Herald "Dallas Times Herald")* and its radio and television stations, [KRLD-AM](/wiki/KRLD_(AM) "KRLD (AM)")-[FM](/wiki/KZPS "KZPS")-TV in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas,_Texas "Dallas, Texas").[[153]](#cite_note-153) The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") granted an exemption of its [cross-ownership](/wiki/Concentration_of_media_ownership "Concentration of media ownership") policy and allowed Times-Mirror to retain the newspaper and the television outlet, which was renamed [KDFW-TV](/wiki/KDFW-TV "KDFW-TV").

Times-Mirror Broadcasting later acquired [KTBC-TV](/wiki/KTBC-TV "KTBC-TV") in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") in 1973;[[154]](#cite_note-154) and in 1980 purchased a group of stations owned by [Newhouse Newspapers](/wiki/Advance_Publications "Advance Publications"): WAPI-TV (now [WVTM-TV](/wiki/WVTM-TV "WVTM-TV")) in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"); [KTVI](/wiki/KTVI "KTVI") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"); WSYR-TV (now [WSTM-TV](/wiki/WSTM-TV "WSTM-TV")) in [Syracuse, New York](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York") and its satellite station WSYE-TV (now [WETM-TV](/wiki/WETM-TV "WETM-TV")) in [Elmira, New York](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"); and WTPA-TV (now [WHTM-TV](/wiki/WHTM-TV "WHTM-TV")) in [Harrisburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania").[[155]](#cite_note-155) The company also entered the field of cable television, servicing the [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego,_California "San Diego, California") areas, amongst others. They were originally titled **Times-Mirror Cable**, and were later renamed to **Dimension Cable Television**. Similarly, they also attempted to enter the pay-TV market, with the [Spotlight](/wiki/Spotlight_(TV_channel) "Spotlight (TV channel)") movie network; it was not successful and was quickly shut down. The cable systems were sold in the mid-1990s to [Cox Communications](/wiki/Cox_Communications "Cox Communications").

Times-Mirror also pared its station group down, selling off the Syracuse, Elmira and Harrisburg properties in 1986.[[156]](#cite_note-156) The remaining four outlets were packaged to a new upstart holding company, Argyle Television, in 1993.[[157]](#cite_note-157) These stations were acquired by [New World Communications](/wiki/New_World_Communications "New World Communications") shortly thereafter and became key components in [a sweeping shift of network-station affiliations which occurred between 1994 and 1995](/wiki/1994_United_States_broadcast_TV_realignment "1994 United States broadcast TV realignment").

## Employees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Employees")]

### Unionization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles_Times&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Unionization")]

On January 19, 2018, employees of the news department voted 248–44 in a National Labor Relations Board election to be represented by the NewsGuild-CWA.[[158]](#cite_note-158) The vote came despite aggressive opposition from the paper's management team, reversing more than a century of anti-union sentiment at one of the largest newspapers in the country.[[159]](#cite_note-159)
